item.title: A greeting from the depths of the voiceless
item.description: Right now, as I write this, it has been 45 days since the internet was cut off. I am frustrated. The days do not pass. I cannot do anything. My morning routine has become scanning for new DNS resolvers to find something that lets me at least transfer 500 kilobytes so I can read my emails. Writing code, browsing the web, digging through GitHub, reading interesting papers from PubMed, or even installing a library with pip have become impossible wishes. My only consolation these days is being able to connect to the internet for a few seconds or minutes to receive a notification from Stijin, or an email requesting to review a manuscript (that I cannot even download the PDF file attached!!). Stijin has become a symbol of the scientific community for me: When I see the notification of his WhatsApp messages, I feel a rush of dopamine. But does it matter? The voices of us, the people of Iran, were cut with the push of a button. The issue is not whether our opinions matter — they never have. The issue is that we are no longer even allowed to have opinions. What should we say? Say it’s fine and accept this calamity that has befallen us? Or refuse to accept it? Do we even exist to confirm or deny? Do we even have the right to protest?

item.title: A tragedy - part 1
item.description: On the 18th of Dey(January 8th), at 8:30 p.m., the hospital pavilion was filled with the sound of mothers crying and the wail of ambulance sirens. Blood was running across the floor. Precious young people, groaning in pain, were lying on the ground because there weren’t enough beds. My friend was shouting at the top of his lungs: “Mehdi… Mehdi!” I couldn’t answer him. I was in shock. I didn’t know what I was doing. My hands were starting to go numb. I kept approximating the wound edges to control the bleeding, but my mind was frozen—almost as if only my cerebellum was giving orders to my hands. My ears were ringing. I was terrified. I had never seen so many young, innocent lives torn apart like that. As I worked, I kept talking to the boy to make sure he wouldn’t lose consciousness. His name was Amir—one of the kind-hearted kids from Sattar Khan. I tried to joke with him to keep him awake. He was sixteen. He said, “Iran will be free, doctor.” I told him, “Yes—absolutely. That day will come. Don’t doubt it.” I tried to move as fast as I could, because there was still so much more to do, and I didn’t want to put Amir through any more pain than necessary. Suddenly, my friend—an emergency medicine second-year resident—called out to me. I told Amir, “I’ll be right back,” and ran to the CPR room. A seventeen-year-old girl was brought in, unconscious and intubated. She had taken a hard blow to the front of her chest—like blunt trauma from a club or something similar. On the other side, her abdomen and chest were covered with multiple stab wounds. One of the injuries was a sucking chest wound. Her heart rate was dangerously low. I started crying—helplessly, uncontrollably—tears streaming down my face. Through blurred, tear-filled eyes, I quickly applied a three-sided occlusive dressing to the sucking wound, but it was too late. Her rhythm kept dropping, lower and lower, with every passing moment. Someone shouted, “Cleaner—wipe the blood off the floor!” The cleaner said, “I’ve mopped it so many times… this blood won’t come off.”

item.title: My Linear Model Thinks Getting Older Makes You Poorer (And Other Statistical Tragedies)
item.description:
So there I was, building a wage-prediction model like a responsible data scientist, when my Ridge regression dropped a truth bomb that shattered my worldview: According to my coefficients, every birthday makes you $0.31 poorer per hour.

I immediately canceled my upcoming birthday party and started reverse-aging exercises. Then I remembered: I had fallen into the classic trap of misinterpreting linear model coefficients. Again.

Let me save you from similar existential crises with a lighthearted tour of coefficient interpretation pitfalls—complete with the statistical equivalent of stepping on a Lego barefoot.

item.title: Computer Vision–Based Intraoperative Measurement of Biliopancreatic Limb Length
item.description: A pilot study evaluating whether computer vision can replace subjective visual estimation of biliopancreatic limb (BPL) length during gastric bypass surgery with objective, calibrated intraoperative measurements.

item.title: Diagnostic Delay and Clinical Course of Pemphigus Vulgaris: An Iranian Cohort Study
item.description: A cross-sectional clinical study examining presentation patterns, diagnostic delays, and treatment outcomes in patients with pemphigus vulgaris, highlighting systemic barriers to early diagnosis.

item.title: Effects of Metabolic and Bariatric Surgery on Serum Uric Acid, Renal Function, and Visceral Adiposity
item.description: A comprehensive systematic review and meta-analysis examining how metabolic and bariatric surgery (MBS) affects serum uric acid over time, and how these changes relate to renal function and visceral adiposity.

item.title: M.D. Thesis: Neuroimaging–Cognition Relationships in Frontotemporal Dementia Using Routine Clinical MRI
item.description: An M.D. thesis project investigating structural neuroimaging correlates of cognitive impairment in frontotemporal dementia (FTD), with a focus on extracting meaningful information from low-resolution routine clinical MRI data.

item.title: PERSiMED: PERSian Intelligent MEDical Document Reader :A Smart NLP-based Framework for Persian Medical Documents
item.description: A deep learning–based framework for intelligent extraction, semantic understanding, and structured digitization of Persian medical documents, designed to automate health insurance workflows and fraud detection.

item.title: Post-Bariatric Weight Trajectory Modelling and Clinical Prediction
item.description: A data-driven project that models long-term postoperative weight trajectories after bariatric surgery and translates these patterns into clinically interpretable, patient-level predictions.

item.title: Retrieval-Augmented Clinical Question Answering for Bariatric Surgery
item.description: A retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system that uses the ASMBS bariatric surgery textbook as a knowledge base to answer medical questions with higher accuracy and fewer hallucinations than general-purpose AI models.

item.title: SnapExplain
item.description: SnapExplain is an Android utility that brings large language model (LLM) explanations directly into your reading workflow.It integrates with Android’s native text-selection menu, allowing users to highlight any sentence in a PDF, ebook, or article and receive an instant, contextual explanation — without leaving the app they’re reading in.

item.title: Unveiling Predictors of Hypoalbuminemia Following One Anastomosis Gastric Bypass (OAGB): A Retrospective Analysis
item.description: A machine learning-based clinical scoring system designed to predict hypoalbuminemia risk following One-Anastomosis Gastric Bypass (OAGB) surgery, enabling early intervention and personalized patient care.

item.title: How to Build a Hugo Site
item.description:
This article offers a instruction that can be used in to build a Hugo Site.

1) Install Hugo with winget Link to heading

On Windows PowerShell:

winget install Hugo.Hugo.Extended

Hugo’s official Windows install docs explicitly list winget install Hugo.Hugo.Extended for the extended edition.

Quick check:

hugo version

2) Create a new site Link to heading 

hugo new site site_name

That’s the official CLI command for creating a new Hugo site directory.

item.title: About
item.description:
🙋‍♂️ About Me Link to heading

I’m a medical doctor and data analyst based in Tehran, Iran. I work at the intersection of clinical medicine and data science, focused on applying AI (machine learning, computer vision, NLP/LLMs, predictive modeling, and explainable AI) to improve decision-making and patient outcomes.

🤓 My Story Link to heading

My path into medical data science started during my M.D. thesis at Iran University of Medical Sciences, where I studied neuroimaging findings in hospital MRI scans of patients with frontotemporal dementia (FTD) and related them to neuropsychological test scores (e.g., MoCA and MMSE and their subdomains). I worked with routine 1.5T low-resolution 2D clinical MRI data, used slice-to-volume reconstruction methods, and built analysis pipelines using FreeSurfer and SPM. That experience taught me how to handle messy real-world medical data and turn it into evidence.

item.title: ApoE Genotype–Specific Hippocampal Shape Alterations in Alzheimer’s Disease
item.description: A neuroimaging study investigating how hippocampal subfield structure and shape differ across ApoE genotypes, using high-resolution MRI and computational geometry–based morphometric analysis.

item.title: Post-Thesis: Advanced Neuroimaging Analysis Using GenAI
item.description: An ongoing research effort to improve brain MRI reconstruction techniques, enabling large-scale neurodegeneration studies using routine clinical imaging data rather than relying solely on high-resolution research scans.
